did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning methods, specifically a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and MobileNet V2 with transfer learning, to classify skin lesions as benign or malignant (melanoma detection) using the ISIC 2019/2020 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and comparative evaluation of a custom CNN and MobileNet V2 with transfer learning for melanoma detection using the ISIC 2019/2020 datasets. It reports specific methodology, training parameters, and quantitative results (e.g., 92.01% accuracy), which characterizes it as a research study rather than a review.

title: Detection of Melanoma using Deep learning
journal: 2024 4th Asian Conference on Innovation in Technology (ASIANCON)
brief_summary: This study compares a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and MobileNet V2 with transfer learning for classifying skin lesions as benign or malignant melanoma. The models were trained and evaluated using the ISIC 2019 and ISIC 2020 datasets, which provided 6,112 training images and 2,040 test/validation images each. The evaluation focused on accuracy metrics to determine the effectiveness of transfer learning versus a proposed CNN architecture. The results showed that the MobileNet V2 model with transfer learning achieved a testing accuracy of 92.01%, outperforming the proposed CNN model which achieved 88.92%. The study concludes that leveraging pre-trained architectures with transfer learning significantly improves performance in medical image classification tasks for melanoma detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on detecting melanoma, classifying skin lesions as malignant (melanoma) or benign. The ISIC 2019/2020 dataset used contained 3056 malignant cases in training and 1020 malignant cases in test/validation sets.
The study classifies lesions into benign or malignant categories. The ISIC 2019/2020 dataset used contained 3056 benign cases in training and 1020 benign cases in test/validation sets.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into benign or malignant (melanoma) categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'image classification' distinguishing between 'benign or malignant conditions'. The models (Proposed CNN and MobileNet V2) output probability distributions over two classes using sigmoid activation functions, with performance measured by accuracy (e.g., 92.01% for MobileNet V2).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019/2020
datasets_used.description: Datasets obtained from the International Society for Digital Imaging of the Skin (ISIC) project, comprising dermatoscopic images of pigmented lesions.

use.training: Used for training the Proposed CNN and MobileNet V2 models (6112 instances).

datasets_used.name: Dermis Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset consisting of dermatoscopic images, initially used before switching to ISIC.

use.other: The study later changed the dataset from Dermis to ISIC due to the larger data size of ISIC.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It compared model performance using accuracy metrics (e.g., 92.01% for MobileNet V2 vs. 88.92% for Proposed CNN) without statistical significance analysis.

ISIC 2019/2020.notes: The MobileNet V2 model with transfer learning outperformed the Proposed CNN model. The Proposed CNN was trained for 30 epochs with a learning rate of 0.001, achieving training accuracy of 87.60% and validation accuracy of 88.87% before testing. MobileNet V2 utilized handcrafted features merged with the pre-trained base model, resulting in the reported testing accuracy.

Dermis Dataset.notes: Results from the initial iteration using the Dermis dataset. The model achieved a testing accuracy of 84.50% on an independent dataset. The study later switched to the ISIC dataset due to its larger size (10,192 images vs 1,000 images).

architecture_used.main_model: Two models were compared: a custom Proposed Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and MobileNet V2 with transfer learning.
architecture_used.pretraining: MobileNet V2 was used as a pre-trained base model (ImageNet pre-trained) for the transfer learning approach. The Proposed CNN was trained from scratch.
architecture_used.input_size: 64x64 RGB images (for the Proposed CNN context in related work description); MobileNet V2 is compatible with input sizes larger than 32x32. The specific input size for the final ISIC experiments is not explicitly stated, but images were cropped to extract ROI.

Proposed CNN.layers: Total of 13 layers. Composed of four convolutional layers (filters: 32, 64, 128, 256; kernel size: 3x3; activation: linear), followed by pooling layers and dense layers. Iterative refinements included adding dropout regularization (rates 0.15, 0.2, 0.25 on specific layers, 0.5 in later iterations) and using global average pooling in the final convolutional layer in the third iteration.
Proposed CNN.activation_functions: Linear for convolutional layers, Sigmoid for the output layer.

MobileNet V2.base_architecture: MobileNet V2 (53 layers, inverted residual blocks with bottlenecking, depth-wise separable convolutions)
MobileNet V2.modifications: Handcrafted features were added via a sequential layer with convolutional layers (filters: 32, 64, 128, 256, 512, 512) and Max pooling. A flattened 1024 dense layer with dropout (0.5) was added. The pre-trained MobileNet V2 base was merged with these custom features.
MobileNet V2.activation_functions: Sigmoid for the final classification layer.

training_details.epochs: Proposed CNN: 30 epochs (final iteration). MobileNet V2: Not explicitly stated, but handcrafted features led to 92.01% accuracy.
training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly stated, but binary classification with sigmoid output implies Binary Cross-Entropy.
training_details.data_augmentation: Rescaling, rotation, width and height shifts, shear transformation, zooming, horizontal flipping, nearest fill mode.

preprocessing_techniques: Cropping to extract Region of Interest (ROI)
Input image size set to 64x64 RGB

conclusions: The MobileNet V2 model with transfer learning outperformed the custom Proposed Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) in terms of accuracy for melanoma detection.
The MobileNet V2 model achieved a testing accuracy of 92.01% on the ISIC 2019/2020 dataset, while the Proposed CNN model achieved 88.92%.
Transfer learning leveraged in a pre-trained model (MobileNet V2) combined with handcrafted features significantly improves performance on medical image classification tasks.

The custom Proposed CNN model suffered from overfitting when trained for extended epochs (e.g., 100 epochs), leading to a drop in testing accuracy, whereas iterative refinements (dropout, global average pooling) improved its generalization on an independent dataset (84.50% accuracy).
MobileNet V2 is suitable for mobile devices and embedded vision applications due to its compact size and reduced computational requirements compared to heavier architectures.
The study successfully integrated the superior MobileNet V2 model into a user-friendly mobile application using the Flutter framework to facilitate early detection and awareness of melanoma.
important_remarks: The study is a conference paper (ASIANCON 2024) with limited scope, focusing primarily on accuracy metrics without reporting sensitivity, specificity, AUC-ROC, or statistical significance tests.

The custom Proposed CNN model suffered from overfitting when trained for extended epochs (e.g., 100 epochs), leading to a drop in testing accuracy, indicating potential instability in the custom architecture.

The study switched datasets from the smaller Dermis dataset (1,000 images) to the larger ISIC 2019/2020 dataset (10,192 images) during development, which may introduce selection bias or affect the comparability of initial vs. final results.

The dataset used (ISIC 2019/2020) is publicly available and balanced (equal malignant/benign splits), which may not reflect real-world clinical prevalence where benign lesions are far more common than melanoma.
No external validation on independent, unseen clinical datasets was performed; evaluation was limited to the provided test split of the ISIC dataset.

The integration of the model into a Flutter mobile application demonstrates deployment potential, but the paper does not report on user studies, clinical workflow integration, or real-world performance in diverse skin tones or lighting conditions.

The Proposed CNN architecture details are somewhat inconsistent across sections (e.g., layer counts, activation functions), and the final architecture description lacks precise parameter counts or FLOPs analysis.

The study claims MobileNet V2 is suitable for mobile devices due to its compact size, but does not provide quantitative benchmarks on inference time, memory usage, or battery impact on actual mobile hardware.
